1:
On Matthew 9:1 — “he entered into a ship, and passed over, and came into his own city” — the witnesses identify the city, harmonize the Gospels, and read a lesson in Christ’s departure from the Gadarenes.

The grammatical-historical commentators agree “his own city” is Capernaum (Mark 2:1; Matthew 4:13), his settled home, though Bethlehem gave him birth and Nazareth his upbringing (John Chrysostom). Augustine resolves the apparent difficulty — Matthew “his own city,” Mark “Capernaum” — by noting that all Galilee might be called Christ’s city, as the whole empire was the “Roman city.” Calvin observes the same history is told by all three Evangelists, “though some circumstances may be more exactly related by one than another,” and Ellicott lays out the differing order, baffling to “the labours of the harmonist.”

The Reformed Gill and the Puritan Henry read the crossing as a response to the Gadarenes’ rejection: having been begged to depart, Christ “immediately turned his back” on an ungrateful people and “never read that he came into their coasts again” — for “Christ will not tarry long where he is not welcome,” forsaking in righteous judgment those who slight him. Henry frames the whole chapter as a display of Christ’s power over disease, over winds and waves, and over devils.

The fathers add the mystical sense. Chrysostom marks that this miracle compels Christ’s enemies “to confess the equality of his honour with the Father.” Hilary reads the return to “his own city” as Christ coming to “the people of the faithful” by “a boat, that is, the Church,” the paralytic figuring the whole Gentile world brought for healing. Chrysologus dwells on the wonder that the Creator, “straitened… in the bonds of our flesh,” deigned to have “his own country as a man… that affection might attach those whom fear had separated.” So the brief travel-notice both marks a judgment on Capernaum’s neighbours and opens the scene of one of the chapter’s greatest works.

Taken together, the witnesses make this opening scene a hinge. Henry dwells on the justice and patience of the departure from Gadara — Christ “will not tarry long where he is not welcome,” yet leaves no “destroying judgment” behind, this being “the day of his patience.” Calvin notes the glory of the miracle: “a man destitute of the use of all his limbs… suddenly rises up in health, vigour, and agility,” the scribes themselves supplying, against their will, the occasion that magnified it. The convergence is striking: a deliverance begun in the soul (“thy sins are forgiven”) and sealed in the body, the inward cure proved by the outward, so that the lesser visible sign attests the greater invisible grace — and the very enmity of the scribes becomes, by Christ’s surpassing wisdom, the instrument that publishes his deity abroad.
2:
On Matthew 9:2 — “they brought to him a man sick of the palsy, lying on a bed… and Jesus seeing their faith said… thy sins be forgiven thee” — the witnesses note the faith of bearers and borne, the tender word “son,” and the priority of pardon over healing.

The grammatical-historical commentators fill in the scene from the parallels. Ellicott and Robertson note from Mark and Luke that Christ was teaching in a crowded house, even the gateway filled (Mark 2:2), with Pharisees and doctors come “from Jerusalem”; the bearers “uncovered the roof” and let the man down (Luke 5:19), the “bed” a light pallet. Barnes and Robertson gloss the words for the couch and the perfect-passive “are forgiven.” On “seeing their faith,” Wesley and Calvin take it of both the paralytic’s and the bearers’, shown “by the laboriousness of that attempt” — they “would never have submitted to so much trouble… if they had not derived courage from confidence of success.”

The fathers dwell on the pardon. Chrysologus marks how “great with God” the faith of others must be, that it “availed to heal a man both within and without,” and that the paralytic, “more anxious for the cure of his body than his soul,” is first given what he had not sought. Jerome reads the tender “son” addressed to one “feeble and despised… whom the Jewish priests did not deign to touch,” and draws the lesson that “diseases are often the punishment of sin,” so the cause is removed before the symptom. Clarke agrees Christ “goes to the source of the malady, which is sin,” and reads “be of good cheer” as support to a fainting hope.

The Reformed Calvin notes Christ granted to their faith “the favour which he was about to bestow,” and that the pardon, pronounced first, declares him the author of the deeper deliverance of which the bodily cure is but the sign. Across the voices the verse sets the pattern: faith brings the helpless to Christ, and Christ meets the body’s need by first speaking to the soul’s.

The witnesses press the order of the words. Clarke notes the rabbinic maxim “that no diseased person could be healed till all his sins were blotted out” (grounded on Psalm 103:3), so that “pardon precedes health.” Barnes weighs why pardon comes first to one who “came only to be healed”: the man, “conscious of guilt,” feared he was “so great a sinner that Christ would not regard him,” and Jesus, willing to show “his power to forgive sins,” worked the miracle in “express attestation” of that authority. Calvin adds the pastoral lesson that afflictions are God’s chastisements and that “the only way of obtaining deliverance from all evils is to have God reconciled to us” — so the sufferer is taught the right order of his prayers, to seek first the pardon and then the relief.
3:
On Matthew 9:3 — “certain of the scribes said within themselves, This man blasphemeth” — the witnesses agree the charge rests on a true principle wrongly applied: that God alone forgives sin.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the accusation. Ellicott notes it echoes the charge at Jerusalem that “he made himself equal with God” (John 5:18), and may have come “from some of the same objectors”; Mark and Luke give their ground — “who can forgive sins but God only?” — which, “speaking abstractedly,” affirms “one of the first principles of all true religious belief.” Clarke traces “blaspheme” to “hurt the reputation” or, of God, “to speak impiously of his nature or attributes,” and observes that the scribes’ unsanctified learning “rendered them proud, envious, and obstinate.” Robertson catches the sneer in “this man” (“this fellow”): “the prophet always is a scandalous, irreverent blasphemer from the conventional point of view.”

The Reformed Calvin presses the scribes’ bad faith: they “accuse Christ of blasphemy and sacrilege because he claims for himself what is God’s prerogative,” yet “their eagerness to slander drove them to this wicked conclusion.” Had they thought any thing blameworthy, “why do they not inquire into it?” — speaking to him “to have it explained” rather than condemning in secret. Barnes notes the word means to “attempt to do or say a thing which belongs to God alone,” which is its sense here. Across the voices the irony is plain: the scribes rightly held that only God forgives, and so, in charging blasphemy, unwittingly testified to the very deity that the next verses will prove — for the One they accuse will read their hidden thoughts and heal with a word, doing what God alone can do.

The witnesses agree the scribes reasoned rightly on the major premise and wrongly on the case before them. As Barnes and Ellicott observe from Mark and Luke, their unspoken charge was “none can forgive sins but God only” (cf. Isaiah 43:25) — a true principle, since all sin is finally an offence against God, whose is the ultimate act of pardon. Their fault, says Ellicott, lay in ignoring two possibilities: that God might delegate such power to a chosen servant on sufficient evidence, or that “the Teacher might Himself be one with God.” Calvin lays bare the malice beneath the logic: had they wished honestly to understand, “why do they not inquire into it?” Instead, “already poisoned by malice and envy,” they keep silence to slander him among their own — the learning that, “not used in dependence on God,” only blinds (Clarke).
4:
On Matthew 9:4 — “Jesus knowing their thoughts said, Wherefore think ye evil in your hearts?” — the witnesses agree the reading of the scribes’ secret reasoning is itself a proof of Christ’s deity.

The grammatical-historical commentators make this the heart of the verse. Gill: that he knew their thoughts “was a clear evidence, and full demonstration of his deity; for none knows the thoughts of the heart but God” — so that, knowing hearts, “it could be no blasphemy in him to take that to himself which belonged to God, even to forgive sins.” Barnes anchors the point in Scripture: “the power of searching the hearts… belongs only to God” (1 Chronicles 28:9; Jeremiah 17:10; Revelation 2:23), so in claiming it Jesus gave “clear proofs of his omniscience” (John 2:24–25). Clarke: by telling them “what, in the secret of their souls, they thought,” he gave “the fullest proof of his power to forgive sins,” since both pardon and heart-knowledge are God’s alone. Ellicott marks the better reading “seeing,” an “immediate act of intuition,” and explains the thoughts were “evil” because, “in face of the mighty works and the divine wisdom of the Teacher,” they assumed he had “wantonly spoken” the worst of blasphemies.

The Reformed Calvin draws the same conclusion: Christ “gives a proof of his Divinity in bringing to light their secret thoughts” (1 Corinthians 2:11), what “was concealed in their hearts” being known to him “by his Divine Spirit” — their offense not merely that a man assumed God’s prerogative, but that they “proudly and wickedly rejected God, openly manifested to them.” The verse thus answers the charge of blasphemy not by argument only but by a fresh display of the very omniscience that vindicates the pardon: he who reads the heart may surely absolve it.

The witnesses make the reading of the heart itself a second, silent miracle. Barnes ranks the power of “searching the hearts, and knowing the thoughts of men” among the things that belong “only to God” (Jeremiah 17:10), so that in claiming it Jesus “gave clear proofs of his omniscience.” Clarke draws the same conclusion: “God only can search and know the heart,” and by telling the scribes “what, in the secret of their souls, they thought,” Christ gave “the fullest proof of his power to do” what he had pronounced. Calvin notes the verse exposes not a wounded reverence in the scribes but a proud rejection of God “openly manifested to them.” The thoughts were “evil,” says Ellicott, because, in the face of such wisdom and power, they presumed the worst — charging blasphemy where they should have worshipped.
5:
On Matthew 9:5 — “whether is easier, to say, Thy sins be forgiven thee; or to say, Arise, and walk?” — the witnesses unfold the argument by which Christ proves his power to pardon by a visible cure.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the two sayings. Clarke notes that, rightly read (the comma after “easier”), “both are equally easy, and equally difficult; for both require unlimited power to produce them” — “every thing is equally easy to that power which is unlimited.” Ellicott sharpens the form as an argument à fortiori: it was “easier to say, Thy sins are forgiven,” for those words “could not be put to any outward test,” resting only on the sinner’s consciousness; it was “a bolder and harder thing” to utter words “which challenged an immediate and visible fulfilment,” yet Christ uttered them “without fear of the result.”

The Reformed Calvin explains the logic: “as it is not easier to quicken by a word a body which is nearly dead than to forgive sins, there is no reason to wonder that he” who does the one can do the other. He grants that, measured truly, “the forgiveness of sins is a greater work than the healing of the body,” the soul being more excellent; but Christ “adapts his discourse to their capacity,” for, being carnal, they “were more powerfully affected by outward signs” than by the unseen spiritual power that relates to eternal salvation. Wesley draws the connection plainly: “do not both of them argue a Divine power? Therefore if I can heal his disease, I can forgive his sins — especially as his disease is the consequence of his sins.” The visible miracle, the witnesses agree, is offered as the seal of the invisible: the cure men can see attests the pardon they cannot, since both alike are works of God.

The witnesses unfold the argument as one from the lesser to the greater. Ellicott frames it as an argument a fortiori: it was “easier to say” the words of pardon, for they “could not be put to any outward test”; the bolder, harder thing was to risk words that “challenged an immediate and visible fulfilment” — and this Christ did “without fear of the result.” Clarke insists both are in truth equally hard, for “both require unlimited power.” Calvin meets the seeming flaw — that pardon is really the greater work — by noting that Christ “adapts his discourse to their capacity,” for, “being carnal, they were more powerfully affected by outward signs.” Wesley sums it: “Do not both of them argue a Divine power?” If he can heal the body whose disease is the fruit of sin, he can the more remove the sin itself.
6:
On Matthew 9:6 — “that ye may know that the Son of man hath power on earth to forgive sins… Arise, take up thy bed, and go unto thine house” — the witnesses agree the healing is wrought to prove the authority to pardon.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the demonstration. Robertson: “Jesus accepts the challenge in the thoughts of the scribes and performs the miracle… to prove his Messianic power on earth to forgive sins even as God does,” the word exousia meaning “either power or authority” — “he had both.” Clarke counts “three miracles… the remission of the man’s sins, the discernment of the secret thoughts of the scribes, and the restoring of the paralytic in an instant,” so that “one miracle becomes the proof of another.” Ellicott renders it “authority,” closely parallel to John 5:27, the Father having given it to him “because he was the Son of Man.” Robertson and the Gloss both note the interjected “then saith he to the sick of the palsy” are the Evangelist’s words set in the midst of Christ’s.

The Reformed Calvin marks that this forgiving power is “very different from… what is now exercised by the pastors of the Church,” who only “declare” pardon: Christ “is not only the minister and witness, but likewise the author, of this grace,” and bids the man know that forgiveness is not to be “sought from a distance” but is “exhibited” on earth. Gill: the cure shows that, though “truly and really man,” he is “not a mere man, but… God and man in one person.” The bodily command — rise, take up the bed, go home — thus serves a higher end than the body’s health: it is the public seal of the Son of man’s divine authority to absolve. (A stray Cyril of Jerusalem fragment on Christ’s willing passion is attached here.)

The witnesses count the miracles stacked in this single scene. Clarke numbers three — “the remission of the poor man’s sins,” “the discernment of the secret thoughts of the scribes,” and “the restoring of the paralytic, in an instant, to perfect soundness” — “one miracle… the proof and establishment of another.” Ellicott and Robertson stress “authority” rather than mere power (exousia), and Ellicott reads the words as deliberately recalling the discourse of John 5:27, where Christ claimed honour equal to the Father. Robertson notes the narrator’s aside — “then saith he to the sick of the palsy” — dropped, like Mark’s, right into the midst of the saying. Calvin marks the deepest point: Christ is “not only the minister and witness, but… the author” of the pardon, exhibiting in his own person the mercy men are slow to believe is near.
7:
On Matthew 9:7 — “And he arose, and departed to his house” — the brief notice records the instant, complete obedience that proved the cure, and the comment is correspondingly short.

The Reformed Gill draws out the completeness: “immediately, at the command of Christ, believing he was able to heal him by a word,” the man “found himself perfectly healed… and endued with such strength” that he could not only rise and walk unaided but “took up his bed… carried it home on his shoulders, in the sight of all the people, praising and giving glory to God.” The healing was no partial mending but a full restoration, attested by the very act that a few moments before had been impossible.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott adds the touches of the parallels: St. Mark’s habitual “immediately” and St. Luke’s note that he went “glorifying God” — bidding us “picture to ourselves the exultant joy of the soul freed from the burden of its sins, and rejoicing in the new vitality of the body.” The man who was carried in by others walks out under his own strength, and the bed that bore him he now bears; so the sign answers exactly to the word, and the pardon pronounced within is matched by the power displayed without. The witnesses agree that this prompt, public departure is itself the unanswerable proof the scribes had demanded — the visible token that the Son of man’s word, whether of healing or of forgiveness, does not return void.

The witnesses linger on the completeness and the joy of the cure. Gill notes that the man, “believing he was able to heal him by a word,” found himself at once “perfectly healed… and endued with such strength” that he not only rose and walked unaided but “took up his bed… carried it home on his shoulders, in the sight of all the people, praising, and giving glory to God.” Ellicott adds Mark’s characteristic “immediately” and Luke’s note that he departed “glorifying God,” bidding us “picture… the exultant joy of the soul freed from the burden of its sins, and rejoicing in the new vitality of the body.” The bed once borne by others is now borne by the man himself — the proof, as Clarke observes, that what had been the evidence of his sickness was made the evidence of his recovered health.
8:
On Matthew 9:8 — “when the multitudes saw it, they marvelled, and glorified God, which had given such power unto men” — the witnesses note the crowd’s awe, a textual variant, and the limited grasp of their praise.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the reading. Clarke and Ellicott observe that the best authorities give “they were afraid” rather than “marvelled,” agreeing with Mark’s “they were amazed, and glorified God” and Luke’s “they were filled with fear,” who add the words uttered — “we never saw it on this fashion… we have seen strange things to-day.” Ellicott marks the significance of “power unto men”: the crowd “rested in the thought of a delegated authority, a power given to men as such, without passing on to the deeper truth of the union of the manhood” with the divine in Christ — their praise true as far as it went, but short of the full mystery.

The Reformed Barnes guards the phrase: “to glorify God here means to praise him, or to acknowledge his power”; the words “which had given such power to men” are “a record of their feelings,” not the Evangelist’s statement about Christ’s nature (Acts 4:21). Clarke draws the contrast that runs through the chapter: “that which to the doctors of the law… is a matter of scandal, is to the humble an occasion of glorifying the Most High” — Wesley adding that “what was to the scribes an occasion of blaspheming was to the people an incitement to praise God.” So the same work that hardened the learned into a charge of blasphemy melted the multitude into reverent fear and worship, even if their understanding of its Author still fell short of the truth the miracle proclaimed.

The witnesses divide over the precise word but agree on its weight. Clarke records the variant “feared” (ephobēthēsan) beside “wondered,” and Ellicott prefers “they were afraid,” agreeing with Mark’s “amazed” and Luke’s “filled with fear” — their cry, “We never saw it after this fashion.” Barnes reads the glorifying of God as praise and acknowledgment of his power, the phrase “which had given such power unto men” being “a record of their feelings,” not the evangelist’s Christology. Calvin marks where they fell short: they rightly saw “the nature of man received great honour in Christ” but, “not aware that the majesty of God was united to flesh,” rested in “a power given to men.” Clarke adds the abiding moral — that “the conversion of one rebellious soul is a greater miracle” than any wrought on the body.
9:
On Matthew 9:9 — “he saw a man, named Matthew, sitting at the receipt of custom; and he saith unto him, Follow me. And he arose, and followed him” — the witnesses dwell on the grace shown a despised publican, the two names, and the Evangelist’s own humility.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the man and place. Barnes, Robertson, and Ellicott note this is Matthew, the Gospel’s writer, called Levi by Mark and Luke (son of Alphaeus) — men commonly bearing two names — sitting at the Capernaum “tax-office” on the caravan route. They mark his humility: he “calls himself both Matthew and Publican” (Jerome), the “more honourable name of Levi” left to the others (Henry, Wesley). Jerome answers the objection of Porphyry and Julian, that men followed “with haste and temerity,” by noting the power of him who called: such “virtue and power… he was pleased to throw on human countenances.” Clarke draws the lesson on “obedience to the first call of Christ.”

The fathers magnify the grace. Chrysostom marks the “excellent power of him that called,” rescuing Matthew “from the midst of evil, as also Paul while he was yet mad against the Church,” and that Christ called him “when he was assured he would yield.” The Gloss reckons the publican’s becoming an apostle “a great miracle,” placed by Matthew “among the miracles”; Remigius notes he “esteems lightly” the danger of leaving his accounts in disorder. The Reformed Calvin sees in the choice “a striking instance of the grace of God,” one from an “infamous” occupation made not only a disciple but an apostle, “an example of Christ’s grace.” Lightfoot adds the rabbinic notice of “Matthew” among the disciples of Jesus. The call, on every reading, is a parable of free grace reaching the least likely.

The witnesses dwell on the sovereignty and timing of the call. Chrysostom asks why Matthew was not summoned with Peter and John, and answers that Christ, knowing “the secrets of each man’s mind,” called him only “after His countless miracles… when He knew him to have actually become more prepared for obedience” — rescuing him “from the midst of evil, as also Paul while he was yet mad against the Church.” Jerome admires the publican who “accuses himself,” keeping the lowly name that none “need despair of salvation who turn to better things.” Henry marks “the preventing power of this call”: Christ “is found of those that seek him not,” and the same word that raised the paralytic now converts the tax-gatherer, who “arose, and followed him immediately; neither denied, nor deferred his obedience.” Remigius adds that, having left earthly gain, he was “made the dispenser of the Lord’s talents.”
10:
On Matthew 9:10 — “as Jesus sat at meat in the house… many publicans and sinners came and sat down with him and his disciples” — the witnesses identify the feast, mark Matthew’s humility, and note the strange company.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene. Barnes, Clarke, and Ellicott note from Luke that this was “a great feast” (Luke 5:29) which Matthew made, apparently “a farewell feast to old friends and neighbours” before his new calling; the Evangelist’s omission of the word “great” is “a noticeable humility,” illustrating his own recorded command (Matthew 6:1). Ellicott corrects the rendering — “as he sat at meat” — so that it is Matthew who reclines while publicans gather with Jesus. Robertson paints the “strange medley” at the table: Jesus and the fisher-disciples, Matthew and his former companions, “publicans and sinners,” with Pharisees and their scribes looking on. Clarke and Wesley describe the publicans as “tax-gatherers, infamous for their illegal exactions,” and the “sinners” as the openly profane.

The Puritan Henry draws the evangelistic motive: Matthew, “effectually brought to Christ,” “sought to bring his old associates to hear” him, knowing “by experience what the grace of Christ could do, and would not despair concerning them” — for “those who are brought to Christ cannot but desire that others also may be brought.” Gill agrees the feast was made “to testify the sense he had of… grace,” and that “other publicans and sinners might have an opportunity of hearing Christ.” The gathering, the witnesses agree, displays both the new convert’s zeal for his old companions and the Saviour’s readiness to sit among the very class the righteous despised — setting the stage for the Pharisees’ complaint and Christ’s great word about the physician and the sick.

The witnesses read the feast as Matthew’s first act of grateful evangelism. Henry notes how the evangelist, recording his own story, “neither tells us that it was his own house, nor that it was a feast, but only that he sat at meat,” preserving “Christ’s favours to the publicans, rather than… the respect he had paid to Christ.” The Gloss (after Anselm) calls it “a meet return for the heavenly mercy” and “a beautiful omen of the future,” that the man who would be apostle and doctor of the Gentiles, “at his first conversion draws after him a great multitude of sinners to salvation.” Chrysostom marks that Christ healed the ill-disposed “not only by disputing, or healing… but by eating with them,” teaching “that all times, and all actions, may be made means to our advantage” — the table itself a place of cure.
11:
On Matthew 9:11 — “Why eateth your Master with publicans and sinners?” — the witnesses note the Pharisees’ insinuation that to share a table is to share in guilt.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the charge. Barnes: “to eat and drink with others denotes intimacy and familiarity,” so the Pharisees accused Christ of being “the companion of the wicked,” inferring “that he could not be himself righteous, since he delighted in the company of abandoned men.” Ellicott notes these were “scribes of the Pharisees” (Mark 2:16) come from Jerusalem, who “would not enter the publican’s house” but “stood outside and watched,” putting their two-fold question to the disciples. Clarke draws the diagnosis: “he who, like a Pharisee, never felt himself indebted to infinite mercy for his own salvation, is rarely solicitous about the salvation of others” — “the grace of Christ alone inspires true benevolence.”

The Reformed Calvin reads it as a stratagem: the scribes “attack the disciples of Christ, with the view of soliciting them to revolt,” reproaching the Master with what “was at first sight base and shameful” — as if to say, “of what use is it that he should be their Master, if it be not to withdraw them… to a holier life?” Wesley marks the recurring tactic: “they commonly ask our Lord, Why do thy disciples this? and his disciples, Why doth your Master?” The complaint, on every reading, springs from a righteousness that despises the lost and cannot conceive of a holiness that seeks them — the very error Christ will expose in his reply.

The witnesses expose the cowardice and the strategy of the cavil. Henry notes that the Pharisees “had not the courage to face” Christ “but” carried their complaint “to his disciples,” hoping “to tempt them from him… and so to bring them over to themselves.” They were “very strict in avoiding sinners, but not in avoiding sin,” of the temper that says “Stand by thyself… I am holier than thou.” Calvin discerns the double error: “ignoring Christ’s office and despising others while sparing their own vices.” Their grudging, Henry adds, betrayed that “they have not the grace of God themselves, who grudge others a share in that grace.” To eat with a man, Gill notes, “denotes intimacy”; and it is just this intimacy of the Physician with the sick that the self-styled whole could not abide.
12:
On Matthew 9:12 — “They that be whole need not a physician, but they that are sick” — the witnesses agree Christ answers with a proverb that both justifies his eating with sinners and gently exposes the Pharisees’ self-deceit.

The grammatical-historical commentators unfold the figure. Barnes: a physician’s “proper place is among the sick”; so Christ says, “if you Pharisees are such as you think yourselves, already pure and holy, you do not need my aid… with those who feel that they are sinners I may be useful.” Ellicott renders “they that are strong” (Luke, “in health”), and marks the “grave irony”: Christ speaks “from the thoughts and standpoint of those addressed,” who, though “suffering from the worst form of spiritual disease,” reckoned themselves “without spot,” so “on their own showing” they should not object to his tending the sick. Clarke draws the lessons: Christ is “the sovereign Physician of souls,” all need his healing, and “it is the most dangerous disease… to imagine itself whole” while pierced by sin. Robertson notes it is “probably a current proverb.”

The Reformed Calvin draws out the scribes’ double error: “they did not take into account the office of Christ,” and, “while they spared their own vices, they proudly despised all others” — a disease “always very general,” for hypocrites, “intoxicated with a foolish confidence in their own righteousness,” never consider why Christ was sent nor “the depth of evils in which the human race is plunged.” The proverb thus serves a double end: it names the Saviour’s true work among the needy and unmasks the deadly health that feels no need of him. (A misplaced Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note on the cleansed leper is attached here.)

The witnesses turn the proverb into a diagnosis. Henry expounds the figure: “Sin is the sickness of the soul”; Christ is “the great Physician of souls,” “skilful, faithful, compassionate”; and the tragedy of the Pharisees is that, like Laodicea (Revelation 3:17), they “fancy themselves to be sound and whole… not because they had no need of him, but because they thought they had none.” Calvin names “the deadly disease of feeling whole.” The irony is grave, not playful: the Pharisees’ self-estimate of health is precisely what shuts them out of the cure. Clarke and others note the saying was a current proverb, turned by Christ against the accusers’ own boast — for the one thing a physician cannot heal is the patient who denies he is sick.
13:
On Matthew 9:13 — “go and learn what that meaneth, I will have mercy, and not sacrifice: for I am not come to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance” — the witnesses note the rabbinic idiom, the prophetic citation, and the declared purpose of Christ’s coming.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the form and force. Gill: “go and learn” is the very phrase “used by the Jews when they are about to explain a passage,” so Christ “speaks in their own dialect” and “tacitly reproves their ignorance of the Scriptures.” Robertson hears “biting sarcasm” in bidding these teachers learn the meaning of Hosea 6:6. Barnes guards the sense: not that God opposes the sacrifices he himself appointed, but, “a Hebrew mode of speaking,” “I prefer mercy to sacrifice” — he is “more pleased with acts of benevolence than with a mere external compliance.” Ellicott: the words assert “the superiority of ethical to ceremonial law”; to withdraw from sinners would have been a Pharisaic “sacrifice,” but “the claims of mercy were higher.”

On the purpose, the commentators stress the “studied reference” to the Pharisees’ self-estimate. Ellicott: Christ came “not to call righteous men” (as they reckoned themselves) but “sinners,” and to call them “not to continue as they were, but to repentance.” The Latin Rabanus reads Christ the physician “wounded for our iniquities that he might heal the wound of our sin,” “the whole” being those who “seeking to establish their own righteousness have not submitted to the righteousness of God” (Romans 10:3). Hilary: “no man is righteous by the law,” so “mercy was necessary for all.” Calvin: Christ “explains that they are contending with God and the Prophet” when, “in pride and cruelty,” they are offended at mercy shown the wretched. The verse thus grounds the gospel’s reach to sinners in God’s own declared preference for mercy.

The witnesses gather around Hosea’s word. “Go and learn” is, Gill notes, “a rabbinic idiom” of rebuke, sending the scholars of the Law back to the very Scripture they professed. Hosea 6:6 — “I will have mercy, and not sacrifice” — sets the ethical above the ceremonial; Henry reads it that “morose separation… was less than sacrifice; but Christ’s conversing with them was more than an act of common mercy.” Rabanus and the Latin fathers read “the whole” as those who, “seeking to establish their own righteousness, have not submitted to the righteousness of God” (Romans 10:3); Christ the physician was himself “wounded for our iniquities” to heal the wound of sin. The studied close — “I came not to call the righteous, but sinners” — lays bare the whole errand of the Incarnation.
14:
On Matthew 9:14 — “the disciples of John came, saying, Why do we and the Pharisees fast oft, but thy disciples fast not?” — the witnesses note the surprising alliance, the rule of fasting, and the spirit of the question.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the parties. Ellicott marks that John’s followers “continued to form a separate body” (Matthew 11:2) with their own days of fasting and forms of prayer, “more or less after the pattern of the Pharisees.” Robertson is “surprised to find disciples of the Baptist in the role of critics” alongside the Pharisees, suggesting John, “languishing in prison,” would not have gone to Levi’s feast on a fast-day. Lightfoot details the Pharisees’ twice-weekly fasts (Luke 18:12), and Clarke their “superstitious fasts” to procure dreams or wishes. Augustine and Calvin harmonize Matthew (John’s disciples), Mark, and Luke (the Pharisees), the questioners acting together — Calvin seeing a “stratagem” to “produce a quarrel between them and the disciples of Christ.”

The fathers read the question’s temper. Jerome calls it a “boastful enquiry and ostentation of fasting,” faulting John’s disciples for “taking part with the Pharisees” whom their master had condemned. Chrysostom notes they “put themselves first, and then the Pharisees,” to weight the charge, fasting as they had learned of John, the Pharisees as of the Law. The Puritan Henry observes that the cavils against Christ “gave occasion to some of the most profitable of his discourses,” the wisdom of Christ bringing “good out of evil.” Wesley sets the time: “then — while he was at table.” The complaint about fasting thus opens the threefold parable of the bridegroom, the garment, and the wineskins.

The witnesses see in the question a fresh alliance against Christ. John’s disciples, says Gill, were “a separate body with their own fasts,” modelled on the Pharisaic pattern of fasting “twice in the week” (Luke 18:12); and the Latin fathers blame them for “siding with the Pharisees their master had condemned,” their fasting touched with “a boastful ostentation.” Henry reads the cavil as “a stratagem to produce a quarrel,” yet notes how such “cavils occasioned profitable discourse.” The harmonists reconcile the three Gospels by supposing the questioners acted together — Pharisees prompting John’s disciples to press what they would not press themselves. Beneath the question of ritual lies the deeper issue of the hour: whether mourning befits the presence of the Bridegroom, which Christ’s answer immediately raises.
15:
On Matthew 9:15 — “Can the children of the bridechamber mourn, as long as the bridegroom is with them? but the days will come, when the bridegroom shall be taken from them, and then shall they fast” — the witnesses agree Christ is the Bridegroom whose presence is a season of joy.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw on the Baptist’s own image. Ellicott: the words gain their force from John 3:29, where John had pointed to Jesus as “the Bridegroom” whose coming was “the fulfilling of his joy” — so the feast in Matthew’s house was, in effect, a wedding. Robertson and Clarke gloss “the sons of the bride-chamber” as the wedding guests, the bridegroom’s companions; Clarke notes the eastern “marriage-feast lasted seven days” in extraordinary festivity. Barnes cross-references the bridegroom imagery (Matthew 25:1; Revelation 21:2) and the coming time to fast (Isaiah 22:12).

The fathers and Reformed read the joy and its loss. Jerome: the apostles, “born of this spiritual union… cannot mourn so long as they see the Bridegroom in the chamber”; but when “the time of passion and resurrection is come, then shall the children of the Bridegroom fast.” Hilary draws the “joy of his presence, and the sacrament of the holy food,” which none lack “while one keeps Christ in the eye of the mind.” Calvin: Christ “apologizes for his disciples on the score of the season,” God being “pleased to indulge them in joyous feelings… so long as he is with them.” Wesley: “while I am with them, it is a festival time… but after I am gone, all my disciples likewise shall be in fastings often.” Fasting, the witnesses agree, has its season — not while the Bridegroom feasts with his own, but in the sorrow of his absence. (A stray Cyril of Jerusalem fragment on the Eucharist is attached here.)

The witnesses unfold the figure of the wedding. Christ is the Bridegroom (John 3:29); the “children of the bridechamber” cannot mourn while he is present, and shall fast “when the bridegroom shall be taken from them” — a first dark hint of the Passion. The Latin fathers read the apostles as “born of the spiritual marriage,” joying in the Bridegroom’s presence and “the holy food,” to fast in his absence. Calvin takes it more tenderly still: Christ “apologizes for his disciples on the score of the season,” sparing the weak while he is with them. So the answer neither despises fasting nor commands it for its own sake, but binds the discipline to the rhythm of the Bridegroom’s coming and going — joy in his presence, mourning in his absence.
16:
On Matthew 9:16 — “No man putteth a piece of new cloth unto an old garment… and the rent is made worse” — the witnesses agree this first parable warns against imposing a discipline too severe for the young disciples.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the image. Clarke and Robertson explain the “new” cloth is “unfulled, undressed” wool that, being raw, “will shrink when wet and tear a bigger hole than ever,” so the patch “does more harm than good.” Barnes: there is “a propriety, a fitness of things” — no one mends an old garment with raw new cloth. Ellicott connects the three similitudes: the wedding suggested the wedding-garment and its wine, and perhaps “the very dress of those who sat at meat in Matthew’s house… from the lower classes” made the figure “palpable.”

The fathers and Reformed give the application. Remigius reads the “old garment” as the disciples “not yet renewed in all things,” the new patch as “the new grace… of which fasting is a portion”: it was not meet that “the stricter ordinances… should be entrusted to them, lest they should be broken down by their severity.” Calvin agrees the comparison concerns “the weak and tender disciples of Christ, and a discipline more strict than they were able to bear,” rejecting the view that the old garment is the Pharisees. Wesley: the new cloth “yielding less… will tear away the edges” — the disciples “not ripe” for such austerity. Gill cautions that “young converts are to be tenderly dealt with,” as Christ dealt with his. The parable thus teaches a wise proportion in spiritual discipline: a burden too heavy on the untrained destroys “that good which was in him before” (the Gloss).

The witnesses draw the lesson of proportion from the torn garment. The point of fact, Gill and Ellicott note, is that “raw, unfulled cloth” shrinks and “taketh from the garment,” making “the rent… worse” — a homely “fitness of things,” perhaps suggested by the worn dress of the wedding guests. The Latin fathers allegorize the old garment as “disciples not yet renewed” and the new patch as the new grace or discipline, “not to be imposed lest they be broken by its severity.” Calvin presses the pastoral application: the figure “refers to the weak, tender disciples and a discipline too strict,” teaching that “young converts” are to be “tenderly dealt with.” The wisdom is the same throughout — that doctrine and discipline must be fitted to the strength of those who receive them, lest zeal undo its own work.
17:
On Matthew 9:17 — “neither do men put new wine into old bottles… but they put new wine into new bottles, and both are preserved” — the witnesses read the second figure as confirming the first, and several see in it the relation of the new covenant to the old.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the “bottles” as wineskins. Barnes, Clarke, Robertson, and Ellicott explain they were “made of the skins of beasts”; old skins, grown dry and “liable to crack,” cannot “resist the pressure of the fermenting liquor,” so the wine is spilt and the skins marred. Robertson notes our word “bottle” once meant a leather one, and that in Spain wine is still “brought to market in pig-skins.” Ellicott reads the “new wine” as “the inner aspect of Christian life, the new energies and gifts of the Spirit,” as at Pentecost.

The Reformed and grammatical-historical voices apply it two ways. Calvin keeps it parallel to the garment: the figure refers “to the weak and tender disciples… and a discipline more strict than they were able to bear,” not to the Pharisees. Clarke, by contrast, reads the covenants: “the institutes of Christ and those of the Pharisees could never be brought to accord; an attempt to combine the two systems would be as absurd as destructive,” the old making way for the new. Gill, similarly, takes the “old bottles” as the Scribes and Pharisees, “settled upon the old principle of self-righteousness,” whom Christ did not call, but sinners whom he renews by the Spirit. Wesley draws the practical rule: “give harsh doctrines to such as have strength to receive them… put new wine into new bottles.” Whether read of tender disciples or of the two covenants, the parable teaches that the new life of the gospel must have vessels fit to hold it.

The witnesses extend the lesson to the wineskins. “Bottles,” Gill explains, are “wineskins”; “old dry skins crack under fermenting new wine,” the new wine being the Spirit’s “new energies” that came at Pentecost. Here the readings part. Calvin keeps to the tender-disciples sense — a stricter discipline poured into weak vessels — while Clarke and Gill hear the larger contrast of “the old (Pharisaic, legal) and new (gospel) systems, which cannot combine.” Whether the figure warns against over-straining young converts or against patching the gospel onto self-righteousness, the governing principle is one: a new thing requires a vessel fitted to it, and the doctrine must be measured to the strength of those who hold it — “both are preserved” only when each is matched to its own.
18:
On Matthew 9:18 — “there came a certain ruler, and worshipped him, saying, My daughter is even now dead: but come and lay thy hand upon her, and she shall live” — the witnesses identify the ruler, harmonize his words, and note his faith.

The grammatical-historical commentators name him from the parallels: Jairus, “a ruler of the synagogue” (Mark 5:22; Luke 8:41), his only daughter “twelve years of age.” They harmonize the seeming discrepancy — Matthew’s “even now dead,” Mark’s “at the point of death,” Luke’s “lay a-dying.” Robertson reads the aorist “just now died,” noting “it is not always easy even for physicians to tell when actual death has come.” Calvin and Wesley suppose a messenger met the father on the road with news of the death (which the ruler, by Chrysostom, may have “exaggerated… to gain compassion”). Lightfoot and Clarke distinguish the synagogue’s “bishop” or overseer from the “ruler.” Ellicott, assuming Matthew’s authorship, gives “greater weight to his order” than the others’.

The fathers read the scene morally and typically. Chrysostom marks that this miracle, sought by “a ruler of the synagogue” amid great mourning, “mightily discomfited the Pharisees.” Remigius bids us “admire and imitate the humility and mercifulness of the Lord,” who “as soon as he was asked… rose to follow.” Rabanus and Hilary read Jairus as Moses or the Law, the daughter as “the synagogue itself… in the twelfth year,” brought to Christ for life. The ruler’s plea, on every reading, joins real faith — “lay thy hand on her, and she shall live” — with the weakness of supposing Christ’s presence and touch necessary, a weakness Christ graciously bears.

The witnesses harmonize and then mine the scene. The differing reports — “even now dead,” “at the point of death,” “a-dying” — are reconciled, Henry and the harmonists note, by a messenger’s news arriving on the way; Matthew’s compressed order is preferred. Jairus, “ruler of the synagogue,” comes for his “one only daughter” of twelve years, and his prompt, humble worship discomfits the watching Pharisees. The Latin fathers allegorize freely — Jairus as Moses or the Law, the daughter as the synagogue “in its twelfth year,” brought at last to Christ for life. The eastern voices note the faith mixed with weakness: he thinks Christ’s bodily presence and the laying-on of hands necessary, yet comes. That mingled faith Christ does not rebuke but honours, rising at once to follow.
19:
On Matthew 9:19 — “And Jesus arose, and followed him, and so did his disciples” — the brief notice shows Christ’s ready compassion, and the comment draws a lesson for ministry.

The Reformed Gill dwells on what Christ overlooked in his prompt rising: he “did not upbraid” the ruler with the treatment he met from men of his profession, “who cast out of their synagogues such as confessed him to be the Messiah”; nor did he “take notice of any weakness in his faith” — that he thought it “necessary he should go with him to his house,” when he could have restored the child “absent, as present,” or that he prescribed a method, “by laying his hands upon her.” These he passed over, and “at once got up from Matthew’s table” to follow.

The grammatical-historical Clarke draws the pastoral application: “our blessed Lord could have acted as well at a distance as present; but he goes to the place, to teach his ministers not to spare either their steps or their pains when the salvation of a soul is in question.” Let them “not think it sufficient to pray for the sick in their closets, but… go to their bed-sides, that they may instruct and comfort them” — for “he can have little unction in private who does not also give himself up to public duties.” The going itself, with the disciples following, both displays the Lord’s condescending mercy and sets a pattern of diligent, personal care, even as the journey will be interrupted by another act of healing on the way.

The witnesses make Christ’s ready rising a pattern for ministers. He arose, Gill notes, “not upbraiding the ruler nor noting his weak faith” — though Jairus thought “presence and touch needful” — but “graciously following.” Barnes and Clarke draw the application: though “able to heal at a distance,” Christ “goes to the place,” teaching ministers “to spare no pains for a soul, to go to the bedside, not only pray in the closet.” The condescension is the lesson — the Lord who could have spoken a word from afar instead walks the road, and his disciples after him, so that the visit itself becomes a sermon on diligence and compassion. The interruption that follows on the way will only enlarge the scene, but it does not divert his settled purpose toward the ruler’s house.
20:
On Matthew 9:20 — “a woman, which was diseased with an issue of blood twelve years, came behind him, and touched the hem of his garment” — the witnesses note her long affliction, her shame, the fringe she touched, and the faith mixed with weakness.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the details. Barnes and Ellicott note the disease was “reckoned unclean” (Leviticus 15:25), so she “was unwilling to make personal application… or even to touch his person,” shrinking in shame; she had “expended all her property, and grew worse” (Mark 5:26). Robertson and Clarke explain the “hem” as the tassel or fringe (kraspedon) at the corner of the outer garment, of twisted wool, worn by command (Numbers 15:38) — Jesus wearing the dress of other men. Ellicott marks the poignant coincidence: her twelve years of suffering matched the age of the ruler’s daughter, his sorrow “sudden after twelve years of joyful hope,” hers “the sickness of hope deferred.” Lightfoot adds the rabbinic background of the disease and the streets of Capernaum.

The Reformed Calvin draws out both the faith and its flaw. The incurable disease, long endured and vainly treated, makes the sudden cure “by the mere touch of a garment” shine “with brighter glory,” “perfectly obvious that it was not accomplished by human power.” Yet her thought that touching the garment would heal her “arose from an extraordinary impulse” and “ought not to be regarded as a general rule”; Robertson likewise notes “an element of superstition in her faith, as many people have,” which “Jesus honours” while curing her. Wesley reads her coming “behind” as “out of bashfulness and humility.” The verse thus sets a faith real but imperfect, reaching timidly for the hem, which Christ will presently draw into the open and commend.

The witnesses set the woman’s long misery against the instant cure. Hers was “an unclean disease” (Leviticus 15:25) endured twelve years — matching, by a touching coincidence, the dying girl’s age — that drove her in shame to touch the “fringe” or tassel of his garment (Numbers 15:38). The Reformed voices note how “the long, incurable, vainly-treated disease makes the instant cure shine”: she had “spent all that she had,” and physicians had failed. They allow her thought of touching the garment to carry “an element of superstition,” an “extraordinary impulse, not a general rule” — and yet one Christ “honours.” Her modesty and her uncleanness alike pressed her to come secretly; faith and weakness are mingled in the very gesture by which she is healed.
21:
On Matthew 9:21 — “For she said within herself, If I may but touch his garment, I shall be whole” — the witnesses read in her inward resolve a faith real yet imperfect, mingled with shame.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh its quality. Ellicott: “the words indicate a faith real but not strong” — she “believed in the power to heal, but did not trust the love,” and “thought not of a will that seeks to bless… but of a physical effluence passing from the body to the garments.” Yet “weak as the faith was, it was accepted,” outward things being endowed with “a virtue not their own” (as later the handkerchiefs and aprons of Acts 19:12). Clarke notes her disorder was “of that delicate nature that modesty forbade… public acknowledgment,” and that “the touch of such a person was by the law reputed unclean,” so she “endeavoured to transact the whole business in private.”

The Reformed Gill marks that “she reasoned the matter in her mind… and firmly believed it, being strongly impressed and influenced by the Spirit of God,” encouraged by report of cures wrought on those who touched him. Across the voices the verse displays the very nature of a faith that, though clouded by superstition and held back by shame, yet truly lays hold of Christ — and is not despised by him. Clarke draws the parallel of the Lord’s Supper: “what more simple than a morsel of bread, and a few drops of wine,” yet, received by faith, they make the believer “partaker of the blessings purchased by the sacrifice they represent” — little things rendered “efficacious to our salvation” through faith in Christ.

The witnesses anatomize the mixture in her faith. She “said within herself” — reasoning, Gill and the Reformed note, and “firmly” believing, “moved by the Spirit and encouraged by reports of cures by touch.” Yet the grammatical-historical voices mark its weakness: she believed “the power, not the love,” thinking of “a physical effluence” from the garment, as though virtue worked mechanically (cf. Acts 19:12). “Modesty and uncleanness drove her to act in private.” The lesson the witnesses draw is comfort for the timid: a faith “real but weak,” shamefaced and half-superstitious, reaching only for the hem, is “yet accepted.” Christ meets faith in its littleness and does not despise the trembling hand that touches only the edge of his robe — for it is the touch of faith, not its measure, that he honours.
22:
On Matthew 9:22 — “Jesus turned him about, and… said, Daughter, be of good comfort; thy faith hath made thee whole” — the witnesses note Christ’s omniscience, his tender word, and the saving power of faith.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the encouragement. Barnes notes from Mark that the woman, “fearing and trembling, came and told him all the truth,” perhaps dreading offense at her touch; Christ “silenced her fears, commended her faith,” and called her “daughter,” a word “of tenderness and affection.” Ellicott renders “thy faith hath saved thee,” saving her “in the higher as well as the lower sense” — though there be “imperfect knowledge, false shame, imperfect trust, yet if the germ of faith be there” it avails. Clarke: “thy faith in my power has interested that power in thy behalf.”

The Reformed Gill makes the turning a “clear proof of his omniscience, and so of his deity”: knowing “that virtue was gone out of him,” he turned not in anger at being touched by an unclean woman — for “such was the power and holiness of Christ” that he could not be defiled, but rather her impurity “was removed” — but to publish her cure. Calvin reads the word “be of good comfort” as showing “the weakness of her faith”: had there been nothing amiss, Christ “would not have corrected it by exhorting her,” yet he commends her, so that “faith, to please God, needs forgiveness, and is at the same time sustained by new aid.” Wesley notes she was “struck with fear when he turned and looked,” the more because she was unclean by the law. The verse thus draws a trembling, hidden faith into the open, and crowns it not with rebuke but with peace.

The witnesses dwell on the turning and the word. The turning, Gill notes, “shows omniscience” — he who could not be touched unawares draws the hidden act into the open. The tender address, “Daughter, be of good comfort,” the Reformed read as both commendation and correction: Christ “could not be defiled but removed her impurity,” and the exhortation gently “corrects the weakness of her faith, yet commends it.” “Thy faith hath made thee whole” is taken in both senses, the lower and the higher — bodily healing and the salvation of the soul. Calvin notes that even genuine faith “needs forgiveness and new aid.” So Christ will not let the cure stay secret: he draws her trembling confession into the light, that her faith, owned before all, may be confirmed and her fear turned to peace.
23:
On Matthew 9:23 — “when Jesus came into the ruler’s house, and saw the minstrels and the people making a noise” — the witnesses explain the funeral custom, the proof of death it gave, and the fathers’ allegory.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the scene. Robertson and Clarke describe the “flute-players” (aulētas) and “hired mourning women” (Jeremiah 9:17) who raised a “tumult” of wailing; Lightfoot cites the rabbinic rule that even “the poorest among the Israelites” afforded his dead wife “not less than two pipes, and one woman to make lamentation.” Wesley notes flutes were used “especially on the death of children.” This wailing crowd, gathered so quickly, was itself “a proof of her death” (Chrysostom), who adds that Christ “proceeded slowly… that he might suffer the maid to die,” so “an evident miracle” be wrought, as he waited for Lazarus.

The fathers read it spiritually. Bede: “to you she is dead, but to God who has power to give life, she sleeps.” Jerome: “to this day the damsel lies dead in the ruler’s house, and they that seem to be teachers are but minstrels singing funeral dirges” — the unbelieving Jews “the people making a noise,” until “the fulness of the Gentiles come in.” Hilary reads the synagogue, through which “resounded… a strain of wailing.” Gregory and Rabanus give the moral sense: “unless the multitude of worldly cares is first banished from the heart, the soul which is dead within cannot rise again”; the minstrels are “flatterers who cherish the dead.” The Gloss notes the established truth of the miracle, “so that it could not be supposed a forgery.” The scene of professional mourning thus both attests the reality of the death and, for the fathers, figures the noise of the world that must be put out before life returns.

The witnesses read the house of mourning on two levels. Literally, the “minstrels” were the hired flute-players and wailing women of a Jewish funeral (cf. Jeremiah 9:17); even the poorest, the rabbis held, must afford pipes, so that the very tumult is “proof of death,” and Christ delayed, the harmonists note, “that the miracle be evident.” The Latin fathers allegorize: the ruler’s house is the synagogue, the dead maid “the soul dead in sin,” the minstrels “flatterers” or worldly cares that must be “put out before the soul rises.” Chrysostom notes the practical wisdom — he put out the pipers but “took in the parents, lest it be said he healed by other means.” The din of professional grief and the silence of faith stand side by side; the one must be cleared before the other can witness the raising.
24:
On Matthew 9:24 — “Give place: for the maid is not dead, but sleepeth. And they laughed him to scorn” — the witnesses explain the saying about sleep and the mockery it drew.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound “sleepeth.” Lightfoot notes it was “very ordinary” among the Jews “to express the death of any one by the word which properly signifies to sleep,” the Syriac reading “she is not dead, but asleep.” Barnes guards against a literal reading: Christ “acted on” the fact of her death and “conveyed the idea that he raised her from the dead,” speaking “in opposition to their opinion” — perhaps the Sadducean notion that the dead “had ceased to be” — by affirming “she sleepeth,” that is, shall be raised “as a person is from natural sleep” (Clarke). Robertson and Clarke gloss the mockery (kategelōn): “they ridiculed him… a loud and repeated guffaw of scorn,” the very people who had been wailing now laughing.

The Reformed Gill and the Wesleyan Wesley take the same sense: it is “only a temporary suspension of sense and motion, which should rather be termed sleep than death,” for she “is not dead so as to continue under the power of death.” Clarke draws the lesson that “people of the world generally ridicule those truths which they neither comprehend nor love,” while a faithful minister, “copying the example of Christ, keeps on his way.” The mockers’ sudden turn from grief to scorn, the witnesses agree, both proves they had no doubt of the death and shows the unbelief that meets Christ’s word — a scorn that will be silenced when the maid arises. (A stray Origen fragment on the Pharisees and Sadducees seeking a sign is attached here.)

The witnesses gather around the word “sleepeth.” “Sleep” was, Gill and Lightfoot note, “a common Jewish term for death,” and Christ speaks it “in opposition to their opinion,” affirming “she shall rise as from sleep.” The Reformed gloss it as “a temporary suspension of sense, rightly called sleep, for she is not to remain under death’s power.” Their scornful laughter (kategelōn, “loud scornful laughter”) the witnesses mark as unbelief that, ironically, “witnesses to the reality of the death” they mocked him for denying. The saying becomes, in the larger Gospel, the very signature of Christ’s power over the grave: to him the dead are but sleepers whom his voice will wake. The mourners’ derision only sharpens, by contrast, the wonder of the deed he is about to do.
25:
On Matthew 9:25 — “when the people were put forth, he went in, and took her by the hand, and the maid arose” — the witnesses note the chosen witnesses, the touch, and the completeness of the raising.

The grammatical-historical Barnes records that Jesus admitted only “Peter, James, and John… and the father and mother” (Mark 5:37) — “five witnesses were enough to establish the fact,” impartial witnesses before whom “parents could not be imposed on.” The miracle was “direct and complete”: she “returned to life,” and (the parallels add) was bidden to be given food, proof of true restoration. Gill notes the crowd was put out partly that Christ “might keep it secret… and to show he did not affect popular applause,” partly because the scornful were “unworthy to be spectators.”

The grammatical-historical Clarke draws the spiritual parallel: “the fountain of life thus communicating its vital energy to the dead body” — where death has come, “no power but that of the great God can restore to life,” so the soul “dead in trespasses… can only be restored” by Christ, who alone “has made the atonement.” Wesley notes Christ “raised three dead persons — this child, the widow’s son, and Lazarus: one newly departed, another on the bier, the third… in the grave — to show that no degree of death is so desperate as to be past his help.” The taking of the hand, the witnesses agree, is at once the tender act of the Great Physician and the channel of a power that none but God possesses: the dead girl rises at the touch and word of the Lord of life.

The witnesses dwell on the manner of the raising. The crowd “put forth,” Gill notes, leaves only “five impartial witnesses” — the parents and the three disciples — the scoffers excluded “as unworthy.” He “took her by the hand,” and the maid arose: “a direct, complete raising,” read as “an emblem of raising the soul dead in sin.” The Wesleyan note widens the view: Christ raised three from death — this child, the widow’s son, and Lazarus — so that “no degree of death” is “past his help,” whether the newly dead, the bier-borne, or the four-days buried. The touch of the hand, by which a man would contract uncleanness from a corpse, instead conveys life — the same condescending touch by which throughout this chapter the Lord heals, here reaching even into the chamber of death.
26:
On Matthew 9:26 — “And the fame hereof went abroad into all that land” — the brief notice records the spread of the report despite Christ’s charge of silence, and the comment is correspondingly short.

The Reformed Gill explains that “though he strictly charged the parents… that they should tell no man what was done, he not affecting the applause of men, yet it was not possible the thing should be entirely concealed.” There was “such a number of people, not only relations but neighbours,” who knew for certain “she had been dead”; when these “saw her alive, walk about, eat and drink, and converse with them, they must be persuaded of the miracle, and relate it wherever they came,” so that its fame “could not but be spread all over.”

The grammatical-historical Clarke draws the lesson of Christ’s self-effacement: in this business “Jesus himself scarcely appears, but the work effected by his sovereign power is fully manifested” — teaching “that it is the business of a successful preacher of the Gospel to conceal himself as much as possible, that God alone may have the glory of his own grace.” He calls it “a proper miracle, and a full exemplification of the unlimited power of Christ.” The verse thus closes the raising of Jairus’s daughter as it closed the other great works of the chapter: with the irrepressible report of a deed that, however the Doer sought privacy, could not be hidden — the very secrecy he enjoined giving way before the plain, public fact that a dead child lived again.

The witnesses note the irrepressible report and the self-effacing Christ. Though he “charged silence,” Gill observes, “the miracle could not be hidden”: the “neighbours who knew her dead, seeing her alive,” spread “the fame… everywhere.” Clarke draws the minister’s lesson from Christ’s reticence — “Jesus scarcely appears; the work alone is manifested,” teaching that “the preacher should conceal himself that God have the glory.” The raising stands as “a proper miracle of unlimited power,” and its very concealment serves its end, for the deed proclaims itself where the doer withdraws. So the episode of Jairus’s daughter closes as it ran — faith pressing through the crowd, death undone by a touch, and the Lord seeking no acclaim, content that “God be glorified” in a work whose fame outruns every charge to keep it quiet.
27:
On Matthew 9:27 — “two blind men followed him, crying, and saying, Thou Son of David, have mercy on us” — the witnesses note the Messianic title, the faith that came by hearing, and the allegory of the two.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the title. Barnes, Clarke, and Ellicott agree “Son of David” was “the same as if they had called him Messiah” (Isaiah 9:7; Matthew 1:1), used again by the woman of Canaan and the blind at Jericho (Matthew 20:30–31); the title shows “a generally received opinion… that the Messiah should be son of David” and that Jesus was “incontestably acknowledged” of that stock. Robertson notes the recurring pairs — two demoniacs, two blind men. Jerome turns the title against “Marcion and Manichaeus… who mangle the Old Testament”: if Christ “was not born in the flesh, how is he the Son of David?”

The fathers press the faith and the figure. Chrysostom marks “no small charge against the Jews, that these men, having lost their sight, yet believe by means of their hearing only,” while the seeing would not believe; he notes Christ “oftentimes desired to be asked,” that none think him eager for display, and led them to a house to heal them in private. Remigius reads the two blind as “the two nations of Jews and Gentiles,” rightly calling him Son of David since Mary was of David’s line, and adds that confession with the mouth makes faith “deserving of a higher reward” (Romans 10:10); Rabanus, that as the Gospel was preached “many of the Jews and Gentiles began to follow.” The blind men’s cry, on every reading, is a model of importunate, believing prayer — owning Christ as Messiah and seeking only mercy.

The witnesses unfold the cry “Son of David.” The title, Gill and Robertson note, is “the Messiah” (Isaiah 9:7; Matthew 1:1), used again by the Canaanite woman and the blind at Jericho — an “importunate cry for mercy.” The eastern fathers turn it into a charge against the seeing: “the blind believe by hearing while the seeing will not,” and Christ leads them to a house “to heal privately, shunning fame.” The Latin fathers read the two blind men as “Jews and Gentiles,” and find in the title a proof of Christ’s “real birth of David’s line” against those who denied his true humanity, with confession made “unto salvation” (Romans 10:10). Sightless eyes and seeing faith are set against seeing eyes and blind unbelief — the recurring irony of the chapter.
28:
On Matthew 9:28 — “when he was come into the house, the blind men came to him: and Jesus saith unto them, Believe ye that I am able to do this? They said unto him, Yea, Lord” — the witnesses note the testing of faith and Christ’s seeking of privacy.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the delay and the question. Gill: Christ “took no notice of them by the way… but went on his way; which he did, partly to avoid the populace… and partly to try their faith, and the constancy of it.” Ellicott and Clarke note he “went into the house” (Peter’s at Capernaum) “to avoid the tumult and publicity,” seeking privacy; and that “faith was the antecedent condition of the miracle” — the cry “have mercy” having implied the request, he now draws out their explicit belief in his power.

The Reformed Calvin adds that, though Christ here interrogates them “as to his power,” “our knowledge of his power will be cold and unprofitable if we are not convinced of his willingness”; yet Christ is “satisfied with their reply, and applauds their faith,” for they had “some perception of his grace,” having owned him “Son of David… Redeemer of their nation and author of all blessings.” Clarke draws the pattern for the spiritually blind: “a lively faith in the almighty grace of Christ,” “a fervent, incessant cry,” and “a proper view of his incarnation.” The question and answer, the witnesses agree, make faith the door of the cure: Christ will do for them according to what they believe, so he first brings their belief to confession.

The witnesses explain why Christ passed the blind men by until the house. He “passes them by,” Gill and Calvin note, “to try their faith and avoid the crowd,” then in private “draws out their explicit belief in his power,” faith being “the antecedent of the miracle.” The Reformed press a distinction: bare “knowledge of his power is cold without conviction of his willingness” — yet Christ “applauds their faith,” for they had already owned him “Son of David and Redeemer.” The question “Believe ye that I am able?” is thus no idle test but the very door of the cure, eliciting the confession on which the gift will turn. The privacy he seeks is of a piece with the whole chapter’s pattern — the Lord who shuns acclaim yet will not heal without first calling forth, and honouring, the faith that seeks him.
29:
On Matthew 9:29 — “Then touched he their eyes, saying, According to your faith be it unto you” — the witnesses note the touch as a sign of grace and the proportioning of the gift to faith.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the touch. Ellicott marks this “the first recorded instance of the method our Lord seems always to have adopted in the case of the blind”: others might have their faith strengthened “by the look of sympathy… in the face of the Healer,” but the blind, “shut out” from that, had its absence “supplied by acts they would naturally connect with the purpose to heal them” (compare John 9:6). Robertson notes Jesus “rewards their faith and yet he touched their eyes… with kindly sympathy.” Gill: he could have healed “without touching… by a word,” but did so “as a sign of his favour… and in compliance with their weakness, who might expect some manual operation.”

The Reformed Calvin draws the general doctrine from “according to your faith”: though the subject is “a remarkable benefit conferred on two men,” we may learn that “if we pray in faith, we will never sustain a refusal in our prayers.” Indeed, he adds, “if those two men, whose faith was small and imperfectly formed, obtained what they wished, much more efficacious will now be the faith” of those “endued with the Spirit of adoption, and relying on the sacrifice of Christ.” The word makes the measure of faith the measure of the blessing — not that faith earns the cure, but that it is the open hand by which Christ’s grace is received.

The witnesses read the touch and the measure together. The touch upon the eyes is “a sign of grace and a help to the blind,” who “could not see the Healer’s face” (cf. John 9:6) — a condescension fitted to their need. “According to your faith be it unto you” the witnesses take as proportioning the gift to the trust: faith is “the open hand” that receives. The Reformed draw a general doctrine — “pray in faith and never meet refusal,” and “much more” shall they prevail “who are endued with the Spirit of adoption.” The word is not that faith earns the cure but that it is the appointed channel, so that the measure of expectation becomes the measure of the mercy received. Here, as with the woman’s hem and the centurion’s word, Christ honours the reach of faith.
30:
On Matthew 9:30 — “their eyes were opened; and Jesus straitly charged them, saying, See that no man know it” — the witnesses note the instant cure and the unusually stern charge of silence.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the strong word. Robertson, Clarke, and Ellicott gloss “straitly charged” (enebrimēthē), a difficult word from a root meaning “to snort” or “be moved with anger,” used of horses and of men fretting (Mark 1:43; Mark 14:5): it is “as if our Lord saw the garrulous joy on the point of uttering itself, and sought by every means… to restrain it,” the strongest term for “displeasure, or the command that implies annoyance.” Gill confirms the cure was instant and complete, the men given “a clear, full, true sight.”

On the reasons for silence, the commentators point back to Matthew 8:4 and Matthew 12:16 (Isaiah 42:2): to suppress excitement and avoid premature acclaim. Gregory reads a model for Christ’s servants — that they should “desire their own good deeds to be hid, and yet… be made known against their will, that others may profit” — concealment “by our own watchfulness,” publication “of compulsion.” The Reformed Calvin grants “there was… some reason for forbidding it, which is unknown to us,” and that the men, “through inconsiderate zeal, spread the rumour before the proper time” — their disobedience “worthy of blame.” The vehement charge thus reveals again Christ’s refusal of mere display, even as the healed men’s eager report (next verse) shows how irrepressible such grace proves. (A stray Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note on the scribe is attached here.)

The witnesses weigh the “straitly charged.” The cure was instant, the “eyes… opened” at once; but the charge to silence is a strong word — enebrimēthē, “a snorting, anger word,” Robertson notes, “a vehement charge to restrain their joy,” that Christ might avoid acclaim (Isaiah 42:2). The Latin fathers make it a model: “servants should desire their good deeds hid, yet have them known of compulsion, that others profit.” The Reformed allow “some reason for the silence unknown to us,” and judge the men, who through “inconsiderate zeal” spread it abroad, “worthy of blame.” The tension — irrepressible gratitude against the Lord’s refusal of premature fame — runs straight on into the next verse, where their disobedience and its motives become the very matter of debate.
31:
On Matthew 9:31 — “But they, when they were departed, spread abroad his fame in all that country” — the witnesses note the disobedience to Christ’s charge and debate whether the zeal was praiseworthy.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott frames the long-standing question: “whether the zeal which thus showed itself was or was not praiseworthy,” answered “by most patristic and Roman Catholic commentators in the affirmative… and by most Protestant commentators in the negative.” He judges the latter “the ethically truer view”: “to obey is better than sacrifice” (1 Samuel 15:22), “better even than unrestrained emotion.” Clarke takes the same side — “they should have held their peace” — yet draws three observations: “honour pursues those who fly from it”; “he who is thoroughly sensible of God’s mercy cannot long contain his acknowledgments”; and God “requires that what a man has received for his own salvation shall become subservient to that of others.”

The Reformed Gill simply records that the two healed men, “when they were departed” out of the house and city, “went into other parts” and published the report. Across the voices the verse poses in miniature the tension that runs through the chapter’s charges of silence: the irrepressible gratitude of the healed against the Lord’s refusal of premature acclaim. The dominant reading honours the men’s warmth of heart while holding that obedience to Christ’s express word would have been better — their disregard, however well-meant, still a fault, since love shows itself best not in “garrulous emotion” but in doing what the Master commands.

So the verse poses in miniature the tension that runs through the chapter’s repeated charges of silence: the irrepressible gratitude of the healed against the Lord’s refusal of premature acclaim. The dominant reading honours the men’s warmth of heart while holding that obedience to Christ’s express word would have been better — their disregard, however well-meant, still a fault, since love shows itself best not in “garrulous emotion” but in doing what the Master commands. Clarke’s three observations sum the matter: “honour pursues those who fly from it”; he who is “thoroughly sensible of God’s mercy cannot long contain his acknowledgments”; and God “requires that what a man has received for his own salvation shall become subservient to that of others.” The fame the men spread, though against the charge, became the seed of yet more coming to Christ.
32:
On Matthew 9:32 — “they brought to him a dumb man possessed with a devil” — the witnesses note the order of the miracles, the demonic cause of the dumbness, and that this is a distinct event.

The Latin Remigius marks the “beautiful order”: after sight to the blind, Christ “restored speech to the dumb, and healed the possessed,” fulfilling Isaiah — “the tongue of the dumb shall be loosed” (Isaiah 35:6). The grammatical-historical commentators fix the nature of the affliction. Robertson glosses the word (kōphon, “blunted”) used of the deaf or dumb. Ellicott notes the dumbness “was a spiritual disease, not the result of congenital malformation,” the healing restoring the man “to sanity rather than removing a bodily imperfection” (compare Matthew 12:22; Luke 11:14). Augustine carefully distinguishes this from the later cure, a caution that “many actions of our Lord are very much like one another” yet are “proved not to be the same.”

The eastern Chrysostom explains the man “needed to be brought of others, for he could not ask anything… the daemon chaining his spirit together with his tongue”; so Christ “does not require faith of him, but immediately healed his disorder.” The Reformed Calvin agrees the man was not naturally dumb but “deprived of the use of speech” by the devil, “held bound” in tongue. Clarke draws the moral emblem: “a man who does not acknowledge his sin to God… returns no praises… may well be said to be possessed with a dumb demon.” Rabanus reads the dumb demoniac, like the two blind, as a figure of fallen humanity, “needing health in every part.” The case thus sets a deliverance wrought without the sufferer’s own plea — pure grace to one who could not even ask.

So the case sets before us a deliverance wrought without the sufferer’s own plea — pure grace to one who could not even ask. The man, Chrysostom notes, “needed to be brought of others, for he could not ask anything,” the demon “chaining his spirit together with his tongue”; and so Christ “does not require faith of him, but immediately healed his disorder.” The fathers read in him a figure of fallen humanity, dumb toward God; Clarke presses the emblem — “a man who does not acknowledge his sin to God… returns no praises… may well be said to be possessed with a dumb demon.” The “beautiful order” Remigius marks — sight to the blind, then speech to the dumb — fulfils the prophet’s word that “the tongue of the dumb shall sing,” the healed mouth being made at last an instrument of praise.
33:
