On Matthew 9:33 — “when the devil was cast out, the dumb spake: and the multitudes marvelled, saying, It was never so seen in Israel” — the witnesses note the instant cure, the fulfilment of prophecy, and the people’s wondering confession.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the cure’s completeness and its witness. Gill: “the cause of his dumbness being removed, the effect ceased, and the man spake.” Clarke notes “the very miracle… was to be the demonstrative proof of the Messiah’s being manifested in the flesh” (Isaiah 35:5–6), and that the people’s remark — “the greatest of the prophets has never been able to do such miracles” — shows “the poor and simple were more ready to acknowledge the hand of God than the rich and learned.” Lightfoot refers the words not to this miracle only but to all the day’s works, rehearsing the dense sequence — the dinner at Matthew’s, Jairus, the woman with the issue, the raising, the two blind men, and now the dumb demoniac — all in a single afternoon.

Ellicott marks that the verse is “intended to stand in contrast with that which follows”: the multitude “gave free expression to their natural wonder, which, though it did not actually amount to faith, was yet one step towards it,” while the Pharisees “stood aloof… having their own solution.” Hilary reads the dumb demoniac as the Gentile world, by “the knowledge of God” freed of “the frenzy of superstition,” the wonder of the multitude being “the confession” of the nations. The people’s exclamation, on every reading, is the high-water mark of popular astonishment — an unprecedented thing in Israel — against which the Pharisees’ blasphemy will stand in the sharpest relief.

So the people’s exclamation is the high-water mark of popular astonishment — an unprecedented thing in Israel — against which the Pharisees’ blasphemy will stand in the sharpest relief. The wonder, Ellicott notes, “did not actually amount to faith” but was “yet one step towards it,” while the rulers “stood aloof… having their own solution.” Clarke marks that “the poor and simple were more ready to acknowledge the hand of God than the rich and learned,” and that the cure of the dumb was itself a destined sign of Messiah come in the flesh (Isaiah 35:5–6). Lightfoot refers the cry to the whole day’s dense sequence of works — the feast, Jairus, the woman, the raising, the two blind, and now the demoniac — an afternoon of mercy such as Israel had never seen.
34:
On Matthew 9:34 — “But the Pharisees said, He casteth out devils through the prince of the devils” — the witnesses note the malice of attributing God’s works to Satan, and the contrast with the people’s confession.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the charge. Barnes and Ellicott note “the prince of the devils” is Beelzebub (Matthew 12:24), the accusation reappearing later with Christ’s full refutation, its presence here showing it was current before the mission of the Twelve (Matthew 10:25). Robertson: the Pharisees, “unable to deny the reality of the miracles, seek to discredit them by trying to connect Jesus with the devil himself.” Clarke calls it “a consummate piece of malice to attribute the works of God to the devil,” springing from envy — “those whose hearts are possessed by this vice speak the very language of the devil.”

The Reformed Calvin sets the “applause of the people” against “the blasphemy of those men”: the people’s “It was never so seen in Israel” is “a confession arising from a sense of the divine glory,” which makes it “the more evident that those persons were utterly mad who ventured… to curse God to his face.” He draws the lesson that “when wickedness has reached the height of blindness, there is no work of God, however evident, which it will not pervert.” The verse thus closes the gallery of miracles on a note of division: the same works that drew the multitude to wonder hardened the Pharisees into a slander so desperate that it ascribed the manifest power of God to the prince of demons — the very charge Christ will meet with “biting sarcasm” in a later chapter.

So the verse closes the gallery of miracles on a note of division: the same works that drew the multitude to wonder hardened the Pharisees into a slander so desperate that it ascribed the manifest power of God to the prince of demons. The charge, Barnes and Ellicott note, names Beelzebub (Matthew 12:24) and reappears later with Christ’s full refutation, its presence here proving it “current before the mission of the Twelve” (Matthew 10:25). Calvin sets the people’s confession against this blasphemy and draws the sober lesson that “when wickedness has reached the height of blindness, there is no work of God, however evident, which it will not pervert.” The wonder of the simple and the rage of the learned, side by side, measure the two ways the same divine deed may be met.
35:
On Matthew 9:35 — “Jesus went about all the cities and villages, teaching… preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing every sickness” — the witnesses read it as a summary of Christ’s ministry and the opening of a new circuit.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark its place. Robertson notes the imperfect “went about,” descriptive of “this third tour of all Galilee.” Ellicott observes the verse is “all but identical with Matthew 4:23,” recording a “second mission circuit” and “the beginning of the section which contains the great discourse of Matthew 10.” He glosses “every sickness and every disease” as “every variety or type, rather than every individual case.” The Puritan Henry treats it as the “point to be proved” — that Christ “preached and healed” — now demonstrated “by an induction of particulars.” Clarke draws the minister’s example: a true minister, after Christ, is “neither detained in one place by a comfortable provision… nor discouraged… by calumny and persecution.”

The fathers and Reformed read the connection. Chrysostom marks that Christ, having “suffered blasphemy… not only does not punish, but… shows kindness,” going about their towns — teaching us “not to return accusations… but kindness.” Jerome notes he preaches equally “in villages, cities, and towns… not respecting the might of the noble, but the salvation of those that believe.” Calvin reads the verse as “anticipating an objection,” informing us “that the whole ministry of Christ is not minutely described,” for he was “constantly employed” in proclaiming and confirming the gospel. So the summary both gathers up the foregoing works and turns the narrative toward the compassion and the mission that follow.

So the summary both gathers up the foregoing works and turns the narrative toward the compassion and the mission that follow. Ellicott notes the verse is “all but identical” with Matthew 4:23, framing the section that leads to the great discourse of Matthew 10, and that “every sickness” means “every variety or type, rather than every individual case.” Chrysostom marks the grace of it: having “suffered blasphemy… he not only does not punish, but… shows kindness,” going about their very towns — teaching us “not to return accusations… but kindness.” Calvin reads it as anticipating an objection, telling us “the whole ministry of Christ is not minutely described,” for he was “constantly employed” in proclaiming and sealing the gospel — an unwearied round of teaching, preaching, and healing that the evangelist can only sum.
36:
On Matthew 9:36 — “when he saw the multitudes, he was moved with compassion… because they fainted, and were scattered abroad, as sheep having no shepherd” — the witnesses dwell on the depth of Christ’s pity and the shepherdless state of the people.

The grammatical-historical commentators probe the words. Clarke glosses “moved with compassion” (esplanchnisthē, from the bowels, the seat of the tender passions) as “a vehement affection of commiseration.” Robertson and Ellicott prefer the better-attested reading of “distressed and scattered” (eskylmenoi kai errimmenoi) — “harassed, importuned, bewildered by those who should have taught them… laden with the burdens which the Pharisees laid upon them” (Matthew 23:4), and “cast down and prostrate.” Barnes paints them “sinking under their ignorance and traditions, neglected by those who ought to have been teachers… like sheep wandering without a shepherd.” Ellicott notes Christ uses the same words of compassion in Matthew 15:32.

The fathers and Reformed name the failure of the shepherds. Remigius reads in Christ “the disposition of the good shepherd and not that of the hireling,” healing souls as well as bodies. Calvin infers “how great was the indolence of the priests,” who, though many gloried in the title, were “dumb dogs” (Isaiah 56:10), “not one of whom Christ owns to be a pastor” — a confusion he likens to the crowd of false pastors in his own day. Wesley: the people “were faint in soul rather than body… they had many teachers… but none who cared for their souls,… no pastors after God’s own heart.” The compassion that moved Christ at this sight, the witnesses agree, is the spring of the mission and the prayer that immediately follow.

So the compassion that moved Christ at this sight is the spring of the mission and the prayer that immediately follow. The word, Clarke notes, is from the bowels (esplanchnisthē), “a vehement affection of commiseration”; the people were “harassed and cast down” (eskylmenoi kai errimmenoi), Robertson and Ellicott render — “bewildered by those who should have taught them… laden with the burdens which the Pharisees laid upon them” (Matthew 23:4). Calvin infers “how great was the indolence of the priests,” who, though glorying in the title, were “dumb dogs” (Isaiah 56:10), “not one of whom Christ owns to be a pastor.” Wesley adds they were “faint in soul rather than body,” having “many teachers… but none who cared for their souls” — sheep wandering for want of a true shepherd’s heart.
37:
On Matthew 9:37 — “The harvest truly is plenteous, but the labourers are few” — the witnesses read the figure of a ripe field needing reapers, and note Christ’s craving for his disciples’ sympathy.

The grammatical-historical commentators unfold the image. Barnes: “a waving field of golden grain invites many reapers, and demands haste”; the harvest is “the multitude that flocked to his ministry… prepared to receive the gospel,” while “few were engaged in instructing” them. Clarke notes it was “customary with the Jews to call their rabbins and students reapers, and their work the harvest,” and contrasts the many “reverend men” with the few who actually “work.” Ellicott finds here “a most touching instance of the reality of our Lord’s human emotions”: it is “not enough for him to feel compassion himself… he craves the sympathy of his disciples, and needs even their fellowship in prayer.”

The Reformed Calvin draws out who the “harvest” and “labourers” are: by the harvest he intimates “that many of the people are ripe for receiving the gospel,” the elect “mixed with unbelievers” valued by God above “the rest of the world”; and though many “assumed the character” of teacher, “few discharged it faithfully,” so he ranks the true labourers as few, using the word “in a good sense.” Wesley marks the season: “when Christ came into the world, it was properly the time of harvest; till then it was the seed time only,” the few labourers being “those whom God sends, who are holy, and convert sinners.” The saying thus turns the pity of the previous verse toward a remedy — not despair at the need, but a summons to prayer.

So the saying turns the pity of the previous verse toward a remedy — not despair at the need, but a summons to prayer. Barnes paints the figure: “a waving field of golden grain invites many reapers, and demands haste,” the harvest being “the multitude… prepared to receive the gospel,” while “few were engaged in instructing” them. Ellicott finds here “a most touching instance of the reality of our Lord’s human emotions”: it is “not enough for him to feel compassion himself… he craves the sympathy of his disciples, and needs even their fellowship in prayer.” Calvin reads “the harvest” as the elect “mixed with unbelievers,” ripe and valued above “the rest of the world,” while of the many who “assumed the character” of teacher “few discharged it faithfully” — so the labourers are truly few.
38:
On Matthew 9:38 — “Pray ye therefore the Lord of the harvest, that he will send forth labourers into his harvest” — the witnesses note the Lord of the harvest, the force of “send forth,” and prayer as the appointed remedy.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the verb. Robertson and Wesley render “send forth” (ekballō) as “thrust forth… to drive out, to push out” — for the office is “not pleasing to flesh and blood,… so full of reproach, labour, danger” that “nature may well be averse to it,” and “God literally has to push… a man into the ministry who resists his known duty.” Robertson laments, “how seldom do we hear prayers for more preachers.” Clarke: “those who are fittest for the work are generally most backward… the man forward to become a preacher knows little of God”; the minister is “a day-labourer” who comes “not to become lord… but to work.”

The Reformed commentators name the Lord of the harvest and the urgency. Gill takes him to be either the Father, “whose are all the elect,” or Christ himself, who “has power of sending forth labourers” (as the next chapter shows) — “a proof of prayer being made to Christ.” Calvin: “as no man will of himself become a sincere and faithful minister… whenever we observe a scarcity of pastors, we must raise our eyes to him to afford the remedy,” adding that “there never was greater necessity for offering this prayer than during the fearful desolation of the church” of his own day. The chapter thus ends, after its long display of healing power, by turning the disciples’ eyes from the works done to the work yet to do, and putting on their lips the prayer that prepares for their own sending in the chapter that follows.

So the chapter ends, after its long display of healing power, by turning the disciples’ eyes from the works done to the work yet to do, and putting on their lips the prayer that prepares for their own sending in the chapter that follows. The verb “send forth,” Robertson and Wesley note, is ekballō, “thrust forth… to push out” — for the office is “so full of reproach, labour, danger” that “nature may well be averse to it,” and Clarke adds that “those who are fittest for the work are generally most backward.” Gill takes the Lord of the harvest to be the Father, “whose are all the elect,” or Christ himself, “a proof of prayer being made to Christ.” Calvin: “whenever we observe a scarcity of pastors, we must raise our eyes to him to afford the remedy.”
